steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.to put: That would be 'ponere' — 'mitto' comes from 'mittere' (to send)
hints.to throw: While 'mittere' can mean 'to hurl', its primary meaning is 'to send'
hints.to mix: That would be 'miscere' — 'mitto' comes from 'mittere' (to send)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'mittam' — 'mitto' is present indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'misi' — 'mitto' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'mittam' — 'mitto' is present indicative 1st person

hints.pono, ponere, posui, positum: These are the parts of 'ponere' (to place/put)
hints.duco, ducere, duxi, ductum: These are the parts of 'ducere' (to lead)
hints.scribo, scribere, scripsi, scriptum: These are the parts of 'scribere' (to write)

hints.nominative: While 'vos' is identical in nominative, here it is the direct object of 'mitto'
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'vobis' — 'vos' is accusative, direct object

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'agni' — 'agnos' with -os is accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'agnis' — 'agnos' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'agnorum' — 'agnos' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'agni' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'agni' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'agni' ends in -i

translation: Behold, I send you as lambs
